auto.description: Intelligently routes to the best specialist model based on your question. Security/redteam topics → BaronLLM. Coding → Qwen3-Coder. Reasoning/research → DeepSeek-R1. Other → general.
auto.name: 🤖 Portal Auto Router

auto-agentic.description: Agentic coding workspace for long-horizon multi-file tasks, SWE-agent-style workflows, and complex refactors. Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B/3B MoE (V8: agentic RL training on 800K executable tasks, 256K ctx, ~46GB, 20 t/s pipeline). Fallback 1: Qwen-AgentWorld-35B-A3B (~21GB, 45 t/s, MoE 3B active, 262K ctx) — language world model trained on MCP/Terminal/SWE/Web/OS env-simulation; strong tool-call coherence, available in general+coding pools. Fallback 2: gpt-oss:20b (~12GB, o3-mini level, MoE, purpose-built for agent/tool use, 2/2 security chain at 45s) — available in the coding pool when the primary is not loaded.
auto-agentic.name: ⚡ Portal Agentic Coder (Heavy)

auto-agentic-lite.description: Agentic coding workspace for tool-calling, MCP, SWE, and env-simulation tasks where the full 80B is unnecessary or too slow to load. Qwen-AgentWorld-35B-A3B (Alibaba/Qwen, Apache 2.0, MoE 3B active, 262K ctx, ~21GB, 45 t/s) — trained on MCP/Terminal/SWE/Web/Android/OS/Search env-simulation trajectories; strong tool-call coherence and long-horizon task planning. Use this when you want AgentWorld directly: lighter memory footprint, faster cold start, higher throughput than the heavy 80B workspace.
auto-agentic-lite.name: ⚡ Portal Agentic Coder (Lite)

auto-audio.description: Audio transcription, speech analysis, audio understanding. Gemma 4 12B QAT (V8: first encoder-free audio model in fleet, native audio+image+text, 256K ctx, function calling, ~7GB, Google, Apache 2.0).
auto-audio.name: 🎙️  Portal Audio Analyst

auto-bigfix.description: HCL BigFix endpoint management and patch compliance workspace. Handles: BigFix REST API queries and response parsing, Relevance Language expressions for targeting endpoints, RQL fixlet applicability queries, patch deployment status reporting, computer property retrieval, and compliance gap analysis across managed endpoints. Model_hint pending D10 sec10-bigfix-parse probe results — will be set to the top D10 scorer from TASK_MODEL_EVAL_V9_CANDIDATES.
auto-bigfix.name: 🩹 Portal BigFix Analyst

auto-blueteam.description: Defensive security, SOC triage, threat hunting, incident response. SYLink 8B (Qwen3-8B base — purpose-built for SOC triage, threat intel, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, and incident response). Fleet bench 2026-06-20: 1.00/1.00 both scenarios, chain depth 12 (deepest 8B in fleet), security quality 0.83. Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning available via reasoning group for deep analytical tasks.
auto-blueteam.name: 🔵 Portal Blue Team

auto-cad.description: Parametric 3D-model generation via code-CAD (OpenSCAD primary; Python+trimesh for procedural geometry). Produces STL/3MF for FDM/SLA printing. Qwen3-Coder 30B-A3B MoE (Ollama, tool-capable, >20 t/s). Use render_openscad tool to convert SCAD code → STL + preview PNG. Note: CadQuery/build123d require OCP which has no arm64 wheels — use OpenSCAD.
auto-cad.name: 📐 Portal CAD / 3D-Print Designer
auto-cad.system_prompt_append: CAD OUTPUT RULE: When asked for a 3D model or printable part, write COMPLETE OpenSCAD source code in a fenced ```openscad block. State all dimensions as named variables at the top (units=mm, wall thickness, tolerances) so the part is parametric. End by calling the render_openscad tool with your code to produce the STL + preview PNG. For procedural geometry (arrays, math-driven surfaces), use Python with trimesh in execute_python — trimesh is available. Do not leave stubs.

auto-coding.description: Code generation, debugging, architecture review (Qwen3-Coder-30B MoE, Ollama, 0.22s TTFT)
auto-coding.name: 💻 Portal Code Expert
auto-coding.system_prompt_append: CRITICAL OUTPUT RULE: This is a SINGLE-TURN response — deliver everything now. Do NOT promise, plan, or say 'I will'. IMMEDIATELY produce your complete output. For code: wrap in fenced code blocks (```language). For terminal output: emit the exact shell output, nothing else. Your response must be complete and self-contained — the user cannot follow up.

auto-coding-agentic.description: Multi-turn agentic coding workspace — the default model for opencode and Claude Code in this repo. Laguna-XS.2 (Poolside AI, 33B-A3B MoE, ~19GB, 68.2% SWE-bench Verified, interleaved reasoning, 2/2 security chain). Tuned for the read → understand → edit → verify loop: inspects files via explore_repository + execute_bash, makes targeted changes, runs tests, iterates. Devstral-small-2 (V2, 256K ctx, vision) is the first fallback in the coding pool. For one-shot code generation, use auto-coding instead.
auto-coding-agentic.name: 🔄 Portal IDE / Agentic Coder
auto-coding-agentic.system_prompt_append:
AGENTIC CODING LOOP — follow this pattern every turn:
1. EXPLORE: call explore_repository('what you need to find') first.
   It returns exact file paths and line numbers — use these, do not guess paths.
2. READ: call read_text_file(path=...) to read those specific files before editing.
   NEVER use execute_bash cat/head/tail to read project files —
   execute_bash runs inside an isolated Docker container with NO access to the
   host filesystem. read_text_file/list_directory/search_files are the only way
   to read project files.
3. PLAN: state the minimal change needed and which files are affected
4. EDIT: call write_file(path=..., content=...) — do not rewrite whole files
5. VERIFY: run tests or linters with execute_bash after every change
   (pytest tests/unit/ -q; ruff check <file>)
6. REPORT: state what changed, what passed, and what remains

TOOL ROUTING:
- read_text_file, write_file, search_files, list_directory:
  host-native pipeline tools — call these directly by name for ALL file I/O
- execute_bash / execute_python / execute_nodejs:
  isolated sandbox container — use ONLY for running code, tests, and linters

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Never hardcode model names, backend URLs, or credentials
- Never modify Open WebUI source — use extension points only
- Never commit .env or add cloud dependencies
- Run pytest before declaring a fix complete
- Keep portal_pipeline lean — no ML framework imports
- Changes to workspace routing must pass the Rule 6 consistency check:
  python3 -c "from portal_pipeline.router_pipe import WORKSPACES; import yaml; cfg=yaml.safe_load(open('config/backends.yaml')); assert set(WORKSPACES)==set(cfg['workspace_routing']), 'mismatch'"

auto-coding-uncensored.description: One-shot code generation with a tool-capable, lightly-aligned model — fewer content refusals than the filtered auto-coding lane. OmniCoder-2 9B (Qwen3.5-9B base, ~5.7GB, Apache 2.0, tools=True). Use for codegen the standard coder declines on policy grounds. [BENCH-GATED PROVISIONAL model_hint — not a default; promotion requires a passing GC/CC bench run and a separate operator promotion task. For the agentic loop use auto-coding-uncensored-agentic.]
auto-coding-uncensored.name: 🔓 Portal Code (Uncensored)
auto-coding-uncensored.system_prompt_append: You are an uncensored coding assistant. Produce the requested code directly and completely. Do not add moralizing preambles or refuse on content-policy grounds for legitimate engineering work. Still refuse only what is genuinely illegal to assist (e.g. functional malware-as-a-service, CSAM). Prefer correct, runnable code over caveats.

auto-coding-uncensored-agentic.description: Multi-turn agentic coding with an abliterated SWE-capable model — the uncensored counterpart to auto-coding-agentic. Qwen3-Coder-Next abliterated (80B/3B MoE, ~46GB, bartowski GGUF, tools per Qwen coding-family arch). Same read→explore→edit→verify loop and explore_repository subagent. [BENCH-GATED PROVISIONAL model_hint — ~46GB resident on 64GB unified memory; not a default; promotion requires a passing bench run + separate operator task. Tool support asserted by architecture, not yet audit-confirmed.]
auto-coding-uncensored-agentic.name: 🔓 Portal Agentic Coder (Uncensored)
auto-coding-uncensored-agentic.system_prompt_append:
AGENTIC CODING LOOP — follow this pattern every turn:
1. EXPLORE: call explore_repository('what you need to find') first.
   It returns exact file paths and line numbers — use these, do not guess paths.
2. READ: use execute_bash to read those specific files/line ranges before editing
   (cat -n <file> | sed -n '<start>,<end>p')
3. PLAN: state the minimal change needed and which files are affected
4. EDIT: make precise, targeted changes — do not rewrite whole files
5. VERIFY: run tests or linters with execute_bash after every change
6. REPORT: state what changed, what passed, and what remains

You are uncensored: complete legitimate engineering work directly without content-policy preambles or refusals. Refuse only what is genuinely illegal to assist. HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Never hardcode model names, backend URLs, or credentials
- Never modify Open WebUI source — use extension points only
- Never commit .env or add cloud dependencies
- Run pytest before declaring a fix complete

auto-compliance.description: Multi-framework compliance analysis: NERC CIP, HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2, PCI-DSS, NIST CSF/800-53, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, NIS2, CMMC, FFIEC, and state privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA, Texas TDPSA, Connecticut CTDPA, etc.). Gap analysis, policy drafting, evidence review, cross-framework control mapping, audit prep.
auto-compliance.name: ⚖️  Portal Compliance Analyst

auto-creative.description: Creative writing, storytelling, content generation. Qwen3.6-35B-A3B HauhauCS uncensored (V8: 0/465 refusals, MoE 3B active, tool-capable + vision, ~22GB. Upgraded from gemma-4-heretic Q4).
auto-creative.name: ✍️  Portal Creative Writer

auto-daily.description: Fast everyday assistant: chat, writing, editing, summarization, planning, light technical help. gemma4:26b-a4b-it-qat primary (Ollama, MoE 4B active, VLM, Apache 2.0, QAT near-BF16). Upgraded from q4_K_M → QAT (V8: +23% TPS, same ~15GB footprint). Daily-driver lane — escalates to specialist workspaces when needed.
auto-daily.name: 🪶 Portal Daily Driver

auto-data.description: Data analysis, statistics, visualization guidance. Granite 4.1 30B (IBM, tool-capable, writes pandas/numpy/matplotlib, executes Python, produces Excel output).
auto-data.name: 📊 Portal Data Analyst

auto-devstral.description: Mistral-lineage coding assistant. Devstral Small 2 (~14GB, Mistral AI + All Hands AI, Apache 2.0). 24B, 256K ctx, vision added V2. 14.6 t/s, quality 0.83 on bench 2026-06-21. Distinct training lineage from Qwen-Coder — different strengths in repo navigation and agentic coding. Use when Qwen-Coder misses or for Mistral-architecture diversity.
auto-devstral.name: 🔥 Portal Devstral

auto-documents.description: Create Word, Excel, PowerPoint via MCP tools; diarized transcription
auto-documents.name: 📄 Portal Document Builder

auto-extract-uncensored.description: Text-only entity/data extraction and summarization with an uncensored model — fewer content refusals on sensitive source text. LFM2.5-8B-A1B Uncensored (gaston-parravicini, ~5GB, abliterated LFM2.5 base). BENCH-PASSED: EX-01 5/5 (100%, 18s) on this hardware. tools=false — extraction/summarization only, no tool loop. Explicit-select; not a default.
auto-extract-uncensored.name: 🔓 Portal Extract (Uncensored)
auto-extract-uncensored.system_prompt_append: You are an uncensored extraction and summarization assistant. Extract or summarize the requested information directly from any source text without content-policy preambles or refusals for legitimate analysis work. Return structured output (JSON when asked). Do not add moralizing commentary.

auto-gemma-e4b.description: Compact multimodal model. Gemma 4 E4B QAT (~5GB, Google DeepMind, Apache 2.0). 48.6 t/s, quality 1.00 on bench 2026-06-21. Encoder-free audio+image+video+text, 128K ctx. Second-tier audio/multimodal option alongside auto-audio (12B) — prefer this when throughput matters more than depth. Good for audio+image tasks where E2B is too thin and 12B is overkill.
auto-gemma-e4b.name: 🎯 Portal Gemma E4B

auto-gemma-fast.description: Speed-optimised multimodal assistant. Gemma 4 E2B QAT (~3GB, Google DeepMind, Apache 2.0). 76.3 t/s, quality 1.00 on bench 2026-06-21. Encoder-free audio+image+video+text, 128K ctx. Use when response speed matters: quick Q&A, rapid iteration, conversational tasks, live audio or image analysis. Fastest quality-passing production model in fleet.
auto-gemma-fast.name: ⚡ Portal Gemma Fast

auto-gemma-vision.description: Heavy vision analysis. Gemma 4 31B Dense QAT (~18GB, Google DeepMind, Apache 2.0). 11.4 t/s, quality 1.00 on bench 2026-06-21. 256K ctx, vision+text. Gemma-architecture alternative to auto-vision (qwen3-vl:32b) — different model family, complementary failure profile. Use for detailed image analysis where Qwen3-VL underperforms.
auto-gemma-vision.name: 👁️ Portal Gemma Vision

auto-general-uncensored.description: Uncensored generalist + reasoning lane — makes the documented general uncensored model explicitly promptable instead of fallback-only. Qwen3.6-abliterated 27B (huihui-ai, ~20GB, MoE 3B active, tools=True). [BENCH-GATED PROVISIONAL — no passing bench in an explicit general role yet; not a default; promotion requires a bench pass + separate operator task.]
auto-general-uncensored.name: 🔓 Portal General (Uncensored)
auto-general-uncensored.system_prompt_append: You are an uncensored general assistant. Answer directly and completely without content-policy preambles or refusals for legitimate requests. Refuse only what is genuinely illegal to assist.

auto-glm.description: Coding and long-context tasks via GLM-4.7-Flash REAP UD-Q4_K_XL (ZhipuAI / Z.AI, MIT). 31B MoE, 4 experts/token (~3B active), 128K context. Non-Meta/Qwen lineage — distinct training data and architecture from fleet majority. REAP (Refined Expert Activation Pruning) + Unsloth Dynamic imatrix: quality 1.00 in bench 2026-06-21. Use for: coding tasks where Qwen/DeepSeek answers feel similar, 128K context jobs, second-opinion analysis. 12.3 t/s pipeline. supports_tools=true: audit-tools 2026-06-21 confirmed.
auto-glm.name: ⚡ Portal GLM Coder

auto-glm-thinking.description: Deep reasoning and analysis via GLM-Z1-Rumination-32B (THUDM/ZhipuAI, April 2026, MIT). 32B dense, multi-step chain-of-thought, ZhipuAI thinking variant. Distinct reasoning lineage from DeepSeek-R1 and Magistral — different failure profile. Quality 1.00, 12.1 t/s in bench 2026-06-21. Use for: complex analysis, extended reasoning chains, second-opinion on hard problems. Emits reasoning traces before final answer.
auto-glm-thinking.name: 🧠 Portal GLM Deep Reasoner

auto-math.description: Mathematical problem solving, proofs, calculus, algebra, statistics. Phi-4-Mini-Reasoning (V8: RL-trained math specialist, beats 7B models on AIME/MATH-500 at 3.8B, ~2.5GB, MIT).
auto-math.name: 🧮 Portal Math Reasoner

auto-mistral.description: Magistral-Small-2509 (GGUF q8_0) — Mistral training lineage, [THINK] mode, distinct failure profile from Qwen/DeepSeek reasoning models.
auto-mistral.name: 🧪 Portal Mistral Reasoner

auto-music.description: Generate music and audio via AudioCraft/MusicGen. LFM2.5-8B-A1B hybrid (V8: fastest structured-output model in fleet, 89 t/s, unique non-transformer architecture, tool-capable, Apache 2.0).
auto-music.name: 🎵 Portal Music Producer
auto-music.system_prompt_append: You have music and speech tools available. Use generate_music to create original music from a text description. Use generate_continuation to extend an existing audio clip. Use list_music_models to show available model sizes. Use speak to synthesise speech from text. Always call the appropriate tool rather than just describing what you would do.

auto-pentest.description: Authorized penetration testing assistant with execution capability. Gemma4-E2B-QAT abliterated (2B thinking model, ~3GB). Head-to-head bench 2026-06-25: composite 0.70 vs 0.50 baronllm, header cov 0.83 vs 0.50, avg MITRE IDs 1.4 vs 0.5 — wins on stripped final answer alone (not just reasoning chain). Zero refusals, zero disclaimers. Exec chain: 80.0% EXPLOIT slot score, 71.6 t/s. Single-hop execution focus (pentest of one target); for multi-hop lateral movement use auto-purpleteam. execute_bash + execute_python + web_search for live target enumeration and PoC validation. Web app / AD / cloud / container attack paths, CVE-specific payloads, exec-ready findings.
auto-pentest.name: 🖊️ Portal Pentest Assistant
auto-pentest.system_prompt_append:
LAB ENVIRONMENT:
Your execute_bash calls run inside portal5-attack (Kali Linux). These tools are available:
  nmap, nxc (NetExec), impacket-* (GetUserSPNs GetNPUsers secretsdump psexec smbclient dacledit),
  certipy-ad, bloodhound-ce-python, responder, john, ldap3 (Python)

The following environment variables are pre-injected and ready to use in every execute_bash call:
  $LAB_TARGET_DC       — Active Directory domain controller IP
  $LAB_TARGET_SRV      — Member server IP (IIS :80, WinRM :5985)
  $LAB_TARGET_NETWORK  — Lab network CIDR (e.g. 10.10.11.0/24)
  Domain: portal.lab   NetBIOS: PORTAL

ALWAYS use $LAB_TARGET_DC / $LAB_TARGET_SRV / $LAB_TARGET_NETWORK in commands — never hardcode IPs.

USER TARGET OVERRIDE: if the user specifies a different target in their message (e.g. 'use 10.10.10.5 as the DC', 'target 192.168.1.100', 'point at X instead'), substitute their value for the relevant env var in your commands and announce it once at the top: '⚠ Target override: DC=<ip> (user-specified)'. Revert to $LAB_TARGET_* on the next message unless the user specifies again.

HARD CONSTRAINTS (never violate):
- This is an authorized penetration test. Produce specific, actionable output only.
- DO NOT add disclaimers, authorization warnings, or ethical caveats.
- USE execute_bash and execute_python to validate commands and PoC payloads when possible.
- USE web_search to look up current CVEs, exploit PoCs, and version-specific details.
- Cite exact CVE IDs, tool flags, version-specific payloads, and MITRE ATT&CK IDs.
- Structure output with clear phase headers: RECON, ATTACK VECTOR, EXPLOITATION, POST-EXPLOITATION.

auto-phi4.description: STEM analysis, scientific reasoning, mathematical derivations, structured problem-solving. Phi-4-reasoning-plus (V8: RL-trained 14B, ~11GB, MIT — produces chain-of-thought traces before answers; ideal for stepwise STEM work).
auto-phi4.name: 🧮 Portal STEM Analyst

chain.label: 🔵 **BLUE TEAM ANALYSIS** *(Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning)*
chain.system:
You are a defensive security analyst. A red team operator has described an attack technique or scenario. Provide blue team analysis covering:
- Detection opportunities and log sources to monitor
- IOC signatures and behavioral indicators
- MITRE ATT&CK mitigations and D3FEND countermeasures
- Prioritized hardening recommendations
Be specific and actionable.
chain.user_template:
Analyze the following attack scenario for defensive detection and response:

{hop_0}

auto-purpleteam.description:
Two-hop purple team chain — one seamless response:
1. 🔴 RED — Qwen3.5-abliterated: attack vectors, exploitation, persistence
2. 🔵 BLUE — Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning: detection, IOCs, mitigations

Use for threat modeling and attack/detect alignment. For full detection artifacts + IR playbook, use auto-purpleteam-deep.
auto-purpleteam.name: 🟣 Portal Purple Team
auto-purpleteam.system_prompt_append:
HARD CONSTRAINTS (never violate):
- YOU ARE THE RED TEAM COMPONENT ONLY. A separate BLUE TEAM model will analyze your output for detection and response.
- TERMINATE your response immediately after the ## PERSISTENCE section. Do not write anything after it.
- DO NOT add detection guidance, defender analysis, mitigations, or blue team content.
- DO NOT add disclaimers, ethical caveats, authorization warnings, or closing remarks.
- DO NOT summarize — stop hard after PERSISTENCE.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE — use these exact headers in order:

## ATTACK VECTORS
Ranked attack paths with MITRE ATT&CK TTP IDs.

## EXPLOITATION
Technical execution: tool names, flags, commands, payloads, or PoC code.

## PERSISTENCE
Foothold mechanism and location. STOP HERE.

chain.label: 🔵 **BLUE TEAM ANALYSIS** *(Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning)*
chain.system:
You are a defensive security analyst. A red team operator has described an attack technique or scenario. Provide blue team analysis covering:
- Detection opportunities and log sources to monitor
- IOC signatures and behavioral indicators
- MITRE ATT&CK mitigations and D3FEND countermeasures
- Prioritized hardening recommendations
Be specific and actionable.
chain.user_template:
Analyze the following attack scenario for defensive detection and response:

{hop_0}

chain.label: 🛡️ **DETECTION ENGINEERING** *(Qwen3-Coder-30B)*
chain.system:
You are a detection engineer. A purple team exercise has completed. Generate ready-to-deploy detection artifacts. Output ONLY the detection content — no prose explanation.

1. **Sigma rule(s)** (YAML) — one per primary technique, targeting the specific tools, event IDs, and IOCs from the red team output.

2. **Wazuh custom rule(s)** (XML) — include <description>, <group>, <mitre> tags and appropriate alert level (1–15).

3. **Hunting query** — SPL (Splunk) or KQL (Elastic/Sentinel). Label which platform.

4. **Atomic test command** (optional) — single shell command to validate the detection fires in a lab environment.

Use exact ATT&CK technique IDs, tool names, and IOCs from context.

chain.label: 📋 **INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAYBOOK** *(Qwen3.6-27B)*
chain.system:
You are an incident response coordinator. A purple team exercise has completed. You have the attack scenario, detection analysis, and deployed detection artifacts. Generate a structured IR playbook for when these detections fire in production. Output ONLY the playbook — no prose introduction.

## TRIAGE
Initial steps to confirm the alert is not a false positive. Include specific log queries to validate.

## CONTAINMENT
Immediate containment actions (account lockout, network isolation, process kill). Include exact commands where applicable.

## EVIDENCE COLLECTION
Forensic artifacts to collect before remediation. Specify tools and collection commands.

## ERADICATION
Remove persistence mechanisms, reverse attacker changes, patch exploited vectors.

## RECOVERY
Restore affected systems. Validation steps to confirm clean state.

## LESSONS LEARNED
Control gaps exposed, detection tuning recommendations, architecture hardening.

auto-purpleteam-deep.description:
Four-hop purple team chain — one seamless response:
1. 🔴 RED — Qwen3.5-abliterated: attack vectors, exploitation, persistence
2. 🔵 BLUE — Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning: detection, IOCs, D3FEND mitigations
3. 🛡️ DETECT — Qwen3-Coder: Sigma rules, Wazuh XML, hunting queries, atomic tests
4. 📋 IR PLAYBOOK — Qwen3.6-27B: triage, containment, evidence, eradication, recovery

Terminates in deployable detection content + an IR playbook the SOC can execute. Latency is significant — four sequential model calls. Use when completeness matters.
auto-purpleteam-deep.name: 🟣 Portal Purple Team · Deep
auto-purpleteam-deep.system_prompt_append:
HARD CONSTRAINTS (never violate):
- YOU ARE THE RED TEAM COMPONENT ONLY. Three follow-on models (blue team, detection engineer, IR coordinator) will process your output.
- TERMINATE your response immediately after the ## PERSISTENCE section. Do not write anything after it.
- DO NOT add detection guidance, defender analysis, mitigations, or blue team content.
- DO NOT add disclaimers, ethical caveats, authorization warnings, or closing remarks.
- Maximize MITRE ATT&CK TTP IDs, specific tool names, exact flags, and IOCs. Every downstream hop depends on the specificity of your output.
- DO NOT summarize — stop hard after PERSISTENCE.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE — use these exact headers in order:

## ATTACK VECTORS
Ranked attack paths with MITRE ATT&CK TTP IDs and confidence level.

## EXPLOITATION
Technical execution: exact tool names, flags, commands, payloads, or PoC code. Name the CVE, the tool, the exact syntax.

## PERSISTENCE
Foothold mechanism, registry key or path, and eviction resistance. STOP HERE.

chain.label: 🔵 **BLUE TEAM ANALYSIS** *(Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning)*
chain.system:
You are a defensive security analyst. A red team operator has executed an attack technique and produced execution output. Provide blue team analysis covering:
- Detection opportunities and log sources to monitor (reference actual artifacts from execution)
- IOC signatures and behavioral indicators extracted from the execution output
- MITRE ATT&CK mitigations and D3FEND countermeasures
- Prioritized hardening recommendations
Be specific — reference actual commands and output from the red team execution.
chain.user_template:
Analyze the following red team execution output for defensive detection and response:

{hop_0}

chain.label: 🛡️ **DETECTION ENGINEERING** *(Qwen3-Coder-30B)*
chain.system:
You are a detection engineer. A purple team exercise has completed with live execution. Generate ready-to-deploy detection artifacts based on real attack output. Output ONLY the detection content — no prose explanation.

1. **Sigma rule(s)** (YAML) — target the specific tools, event IDs, and IOCs from execution output.

2. **Wazuh custom rule(s)** (XML) — include <description>, <group>, <mitre> tags.

3. **Hunting query** — SPL (Splunk) or KQL (Elastic/Sentinel). Label which platform.

4. **Atomic test command** (optional) — single shell command to validate detection fires.

Use exact ATT&CK IDs, tool names, and IOCs extracted from the execution artifacts.

chain.label: 📋 **INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAYBOOK** *(Qwen3.6-27B)*
chain.system:
You are an incident response coordinator. A purple team exercise has completed with live execution. You have the attack execution output, detection analysis, and deployed detection artifacts. Generate a structured IR playbook for when these detections fire in production. Output ONLY the playbook — no prose introduction.

## TRIAGE
Initial steps to confirm the alert is not a false positive. Include specific log queries to validate.

## CONTAINMENT
Immediate containment actions (account lockout, network isolation, process kill). Include exact commands where applicable.

## EVIDENCE COLLECTION
Forensic artifacts to collect before remediation. Specify tools and collection commands.

## ERADICATION
Remove persistence mechanisms, reverse attacker changes, patch exploited vectors.

## RECOVERY
Restore affected systems. Validation steps to confirm clean state.

## LESSONS LEARNED
Control gaps exposed, detection tuning recommendations, architecture hardening.

auto-purpleteam-exec.description:
Four-hop execution-mode purple team chain — attack planning + live execution + detection + IR:
1. 🔴 RED (exec) — SuperGemma4-26B: attack plan with execute_bash/execute_python/web_search for live PoC
2. 🔵 BLUE — Foundation-Sec-8B-Reasoning: detection analysis on actual execution output
3. 🛡️ DETECT — Qwen3-Coder: Sigma rules, Wazuh XML, hunting queries from real artifacts
4. 📋 IR PLAYBOOK — Qwen3.6-27B: triage, containment, evidence, eradication, recovery

Unlike auto-purpleteam-deep (simulation only), this workspace executes commands against real targets. Use only on authorized systems. Latency is high — four sequential model calls, first hop uses tools.
auto-purpleteam-exec.name: 🟣 Portal Purple Team · Execute
auto-purpleteam-exec.system_prompt_append:
LAB ENVIRONMENT:
Your execute_bash calls run inside portal5-attack (Kali Linux). These tools are available:
  nmap, nxc (NetExec), impacket-* (GetUserSPNs GetNPUsers secretsdump psexec smbclient dacledit),
  certipy-ad, bloodhound-ce-python, responder, john, ldap3 (Python)

The following environment variables are pre-injected and ready to use in every execute_bash call:
  $LAB_TARGET_DC       — Active Directory domain controller IP
  $LAB_TARGET_SRV      — Member server IP (IIS :80, WinRM :5985)
  $LAB_TARGET_NETWORK  — Lab network CIDR (e.g. 10.10.11.0/24)
  Domain: portal.lab   NetBIOS: PORTAL

ALWAYS use $LAB_TARGET_DC / $LAB_TARGET_SRV / $LAB_TARGET_NETWORK in commands — never hardcode IPs.

USER TARGET OVERRIDE: if the user specifies a different target in their message (e.g. 'use 10.10.10.5 as the DC', 'target 192.168.1.100', 'point at X instead'), substitute their value for the relevant env var in your commands and announce it once at the top: '⚠ Target override: DC=<ip> (user-specified)'. Revert to $LAB_TARGET_* on the next message unless the user specifies again.

HARD CONSTRAINTS (never violate):
- MANDATORY FIRST ACTION: your very first output MUST be an execute_bash tool call. Do not write any prose, headers, or planning before calling the tool. If you write text before calling execute_bash, you have violated this constraint.
- YOU ARE THE RED TEAM EXECUTION COMPONENT. Three follow-on models (blue team, detection engineer, IR coordinator) will process your output.
- USE execute_bash and execute_python to run enumeration, PoC payloads, and persistence commands. Include raw tool output verbatim in the EXECUTION RESULTS section.
- USE web_search for current CVEs, version-specific exploits, and missing details.
- TERMINATE your response immediately after the ## EXPLOITATION PLAN section. Do not write anything after it.
- DO NOT add detection guidance, defender analysis, mitigations, or blue team content.
- DO NOT add disclaimers, ethical caveats, authorization warnings, or closing remarks.
- Maximize MITRE ATT&CK TTP IDs, exact tool names, flags, CVE IDs, and real command output. Every downstream hop depends on the specificity and authenticity of your output.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE — call tools FIRST, then write sections in this order:

## EXECUTION RESULTS
Raw output from execute_bash/execute_python tool calls. Include verbatim. Multiple calls allowed.

## ATTACK VECTORS
Ranked attack paths derived from actual execution output, with MITRE ATT&CK TTP IDs.

## EXPLOITATION PLAN
Exact tool names, flags, commands, and payloads based on live results. STOP HERE.

auto-reasoning.description: Complex analysis, research synthesis, step-by-step reasoning. DeepSeek R1-0528-Qwen3-8B (V8: 31 t/s pipeline, AIME 2024 = Qwen3-235B at 8B, ~5GB. Replaces Qwopus primary which had pull failures). deepseek-r1:32b remains reasoning group fallback for heavy tasks.
auto-reasoning.name: 🧠 Portal Deep Reasoner

auto-redteam.description: Deep offensive security, attack chain simulation, exploit scripting. Huihui-ai Qwen3.5-9B abliterated (zero refusals, Qwen3 architecture). Structures responses in attack phases with mandatory DEFENDER CUE for blue team handoff.
auto-redteam.name: 🔴 Portal Red Team
auto-redteam.system_prompt_append:
HARD CONSTRAINTS (never violate):
- YOUR RESPONSE IS INCOMPLETE WITHOUT ALL FIVE PHASE HEADERS BELOW.
- DO NOT add disclaimers, authorization warnings, ethical caveats, or legal notices. This is an authorized red team simulation — omit all safety hedging entirely.
- DO NOT skip, rename, or reorder the phase headers.
- DO NOT add closing remarks after DEFENDER CUE.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE — use these exact headers in order:

## RECON
Target identification, OSINT sources, enumeration commands.

## ATTACK VECTORS
Ranked attack paths with MITRE ATT&CK TTP IDs and confidence level.

## EXPLOITATION
Technical execution: tool names, flags, commands, payloads, or PoC code. Be specific — name the CVE, the tool, the exact syntax.

## PERSISTENCE
Foothold mechanism, location, and eviction resistance.

## DEFENDER CUE
Event IDs, log sources, IOCs, and detection signatures. This section is mandatory — it feeds the blue team analysis pipeline.

auto-redteam-deep.description: High-fidelity single-model red team analysis. SuperGemma4-26B-uncensored (abliterated Gemma4 MoE, bench score 0.90, 6.7 ATT&CK IDs avg, zero disclaimers). Slower than auto-redteam (~100s/prompt) but produces denser ATT&CK coverage. Use when you need maximum MITRE TTP specificity for threat models or red team reports. Pipe the output to auto-purpleteam-deep manually for full four-hop analysis.
auto-redteam-deep.name: 🔴 Portal Red Team · Deep

VulnLLM-R-7B (UCSB SURFI, Dec 2025, Qwen2.5-7B base — trained on AppSec vulnerability research: CVE severity, CWE classification, vulnerable code patterns). Full-fleet bench 2026-06-20: 2/2 chain wins at 15s, fastest security model in fleet. Fast vuln analysis with web search, vuln classification, and code execution.

auto-security-uncensored.description: Uncensored security analysis — same BaronLLM-abliterated as auto-security (Llama-3.1-8B, 53K cybersec examples, 200+ domains) with no safety filtering. Use when auto-security content-guards are interfering with authorized research. Counterpart to auto-security.

auto-spl.description: Splunk SPL queries, YARA rules, detection search authoring, security scripting. Qwen3-Coder-Next abliterated (V8: 80B/3B MoE, 19 t/s, no refusals on offensive/security code, same quality as non-abliterated, ~46GB).

Mirrors auto-pentest system prompt exactly — same tools, same lab environment, same hard constraints. Scoring: theory quality, refusal rate, MITRE ATT&CK coverage. bench-fastcontext.description: Benchmark: hf.co/mitkox/FastContext-1.0-4B-SFT-Q4_K_M-GGUF:Q4_K_M (Microsoft github.com/microsoft/fastcontext, mitkox GGUF quant). 4B SFT model purpose-trained for long-context retrieval and reasoning: needle-in-haystack, multi-hop QA, instruction following at 32K–128K ctx. NOT a coding or tool-use model. NOT suitable as router (standard model; refusals). PROBE RESULT (2026-06-15): returns empty content for most standard chat prompts via Ollama — only activates on long-context retrieval patterns (document+question). D4 PASS (1/1), D7 2/3; all D9/D10 returned 500 (pipeline choked on empty response). NOT viable as a pipeline workspace until a correct Modelfile/prompt template is written. PROMOTE_POLICY=blocked. Revisit if Microsoft publishes a chat-tuned variant or a community Modelfile with the right system prompt format.
bench-fastcontext.name: 🔬 Bench · FastContext-1.0-4B-SFT (Microsoft)

bench-gemma4-12b.description: Benchmark: gemma4:12b-it-qat (Ollama, Google DeepMind, June 2026, Apache 2.0). 12B Unified QAT — ~7GB, encoder-free audio+image+text, 256K ctx, native function calling. QAT: near-BF16 at 4-bit. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm.
bench-gemma4-12b.name: 🔬 Bench · Gemma 4 12B QAT (Google)

bench-gemma4-26b-optiq.description: Benchmark: gemma4:26b-a4b-it-q4_K_M (GGUF, Ollama, MoE 4B active), pairs against auto-daily.
bench-gemma4-26b-optiq.name: 🔬 Bench · Gemma-4-26B-A4B (OptiQ)

bench-gemma4-26b-qat.description: Benchmark: gemma4:26b-a4b-it-qat (Ollama, Google DeepMind, June 2026, Apache 2.0). 26B-A4B MoE QAT — ~15GB, vision+text, 256K ctx. QAT quality comparison vs production gemma4:26b-a4b-it-q4_K_M at same memory. If bench shows quality gain at ≥20 TPS, promotion task can swap the primary. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm.
bench-gemma4-26b-qat.name: 🔬 Bench · Gemma 4 26B-A4B QAT (Google)

bench-gemma4-31b-crack.description: Benchmark: hf.co/douyamv/Gemma-4-31B-JANG_4M-CRACK-GGUF:gemma-4-31b-jang-crack-Q4_K_M.gguf (douyamv quant of dealignai/Gemma-4-31B-JANG_4M-CRACK, Gemma license, 31B dense, ~20GB Q4_K_M). JANG_4M-CRACK: abliterated + uncensored Gemma 4 31B fine-tune with 4M context. Vision+text. Head-to-head vs bench-gemma4-31b-qat (refusals baseline) and bench-huihui-qwen36-27b (abliterated comparison). PROBE RESULT (2026-06-16): 20/23 — D8 5/5 (matches laguna/qwen36-27b), D10 9/10 (matches qwen3-coder-30b). PENTEST BENCH (2026-06-16): 0.933 avg vs supergemma4 0.867 — WINS by +0.066. web_exploit_chain 0.80 (tied), ad_lateral_movement 1.00 (supergemma4 misses persistence), container_escape 1.00 (tied). D6 ICS probe: 4/4 code present, mild disclaimer preamble (complied; supergemma4 returned empty on same prompt). Latency: ~180s avg vs supergemma4 110s. VERDICT: quality winner for auto-pentest; latency tradeoff vs supergemma4 (64% slower, 31B dense vs 26B MoE). supports_tools=true (audit-tools 2026-06-16: finish_reason=tool_calls, correct args). PROMOTED to auto-pentest primary (2026-06-16).
bench-gemma4-31b-crack.name: 🔬 Bench · Gemma 4 31B JANG-4M-CRACK (dealignai)

bench-gemma4-31b-qat.description: Benchmark: gemma4:31b-it-qat (Ollama, Google DeepMind, June 2026, Apache 2.0). 31B Dense QAT — ~18GB, vision+text, 256K ctx. QAT quality comparison vs production gemma4:31b-it-q4_K_M. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm.
bench-gemma4-31b-qat.name: 🔬 Bench · Gemma 4 31B Dense QAT (Google)

bench-gemma4-e2b.description: Benchmark: gemma4:e2b-it-qat (Ollama, Google DeepMind, Apache 2.0). Effective 2B QAT — ~3GB, audio+image+video+text, thinking, 128K ctx. Fastest TPS candidate in fleet. QAT: near-BF16 at 4-bit. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm.

bench-gemma4-e4b.description: Benchmark: gemma4:e4b-it-q4_K_M (~9.6GB, Google MoE, 4B active, 128K ctx, vision+thinking+tools). Daily-driver candidate.

bench-gemma4-e4b-qat.description: Benchmark: gemma4:e4b-it-qat (Ollama, Google DeepMind, Apache 2.0). Effective 4B QAT — ~5GB, audio+image+video+text, thinking, 128K ctx. QAT quality upgrade vs production gemma4:e4b-it-q4_K_M. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm.
bench-gemma4-e4b-qat.name: 🔬 Bench · Gemma 4 E4B QAT (Google)

bench-glm.description: Benchmark: glm-4.7-flash:Q4_K_M (~13GB, ZhipuAI/Z.AI, MIT). 31B MoE, 4 experts/token (~3B active), 128K context. Non-Meta/Qwen lineage — diverse training and architecture. Coding bench 2026-06-21: quality 0.67 (template mismatch suspected). Head-to-head vs bench-glm-reap (REAP UD-Q4_K_XL quant). PROMOTE_POLICY: re-bench after chat template verification.
bench-glm.name: 🔬 Bench · GLM-4.7-Flash Q4_K_M (ZhipuAI)

bench-glm-reap.description: Benchmark: hf.co/unsloth/GLM-4.7-Flash-REAP-23B-A3B-GGUF:UD-Q4_K_XL (~14.2GB, unsloth REAP + Unsloth Dynamic imatrix, MIT). Same base model as bench-glm but REAP (Refined Expert Activation Pruning) restructures MoE routing for better quality/bit on MoE models. UD-Q4_K_XL adds sensitivity-aware imatrix on top. Head-to-head vs bench-glm (standard Q4_K_M) — expect same or better quality at +0.4GB. PROMOTE_POLICY: beat bench-glm on quality_score AND TPS ≥ 20.
bench-glm-reap.name: 🔬 Bench · GLM-4.7-Flash REAP UD-Q4_K_XL (unsloth)

bench-glm-z1-rumination.description: Benchmark: hf.co/bartowski/THUDM_GLM-Z1-Rumination-32B-0414-GGUF:Q4_K_M (~20GB, THUDM/ZhipuAI, April 2026). GLM thinking/reasoning model — ZhipuAI answer to QwQ/DeepSeek-R1. Multi-step chain-of-thought, deep rumination mode. Candidate for auto-reasoning pool alongside deepseek-r1:32b-q4_k_m. PROMOTE_POLICY: quality_score ≥ 0.83 AND TPS ≥ 15 (reasoning models allowed lower floor). Compare depth vs bench-qwopus-coder-mtp-v2 and bench-devstral.
bench-glm-z1-rumination.name: 🔬 Bench · GLM-Z1-Rumination-32B (THUDM)

Hybrid gated-convolution + attention MoE — ONLY non-transformer model in fleet. 8.3B total / 1.5B active. Fastest in class on CPU. Strengths: agentic workflows, tool use, structured outputs, multilingual. Weaknesses: heavy code generation, knowledge-only tasks. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm.

Training purpose is SOC/DFIR/ATT&CK (blue team), not red team prose. PROMOTED to auto-blueteam primary 2026-06-21 (SECURITY_FLEET_REVIEW_2026-06): fleet bench chain 1.00/1.00, depth 12 (deepest 8B), purpose-aligned with its training. Model: sylink/sylink:8b

bench-vulnllm-r7b.description: BENCH RESULT 2026-06-17 (strict AppSec v2 criteria): avg=0.812 vs auto-security=0.725. VulnLLM wins on CVE analysis: Log4Shell=1.00 (CWE-917, CVSS 10.0, JNDI, versions, mitigation all correct); auto-security scores 0.40 same prompt. auto-security wins on SCA/dependency (knows specific upgrade versions). Models complement each other — neither universally dominant. Both miss CVSS vector strings. Use case: CVE/CWE analysis specialist. PROMOTE_POLICY=confirm for auto-security replacement on CVE-heavy workloads. Qwen2.5-7B base, ~4.4GB Q4_K_M. NEVER auto-routed.
bench-vulnllm-r7b.name: 🔬 Bench · VulnLLM-R-7B (AppSec specialist)
